title: Unit 1 Project - Alcohol Use Disorder in Adolescents
text:
This group project was for PSYC 399 - Data 2 in the spring semester of 2025. With my team, we were tasked with finding our own dataset (or combining datasets into one), wrangling the data, and analyzing the data. As this is the first group project of the semester, it was mostly focused on creating graphics with GGPlot to find correlations.
I also took a leadership position and worked on coordinating the team, delegating efforts, and communicating with the instructional team for feedback and assistance. As a result, we developed the following report, receiving a nearly-perfect grade.

title: Unit 1 Project - Alcohol Use Disorder in Adolescents
text:
Overview
For our project, we decided to look at a topic based in social sciences due to a connection to our majors and interest areas. We focused on drug abuse and found a data set concerning the substance abuse disorders in the 50 states and District of Columbia. We decided to look at alcohol use disorder primarily, with a secondary analysis comparing alcohol and marijuana use.

title: Unit 1 Project - Alcohol Use Disorder in Adolescents
text:
Data Wrangling


Show code
# Load resources
library(tidyverse)

d <- read.csv("data/drugs.csv")


We found a data source through corgis.edu, a site Felix had worked with in the past. This source had more than enough observations, so we did not have to combine multiple sources and only had to download it as a CSV file and load it into RStudio.
From there, we filtered the dataset to keep only years 2018, 2013, and 2008. We also dropped most of the columns, keeping only “State,” “Year,” “Rates.Alcohol.Use.Disorder.Past.Year.12.17”, “Rates.Alcohol.Use.Disorder.Past.Year.18.25”, and “Rates.Marijuana.Used.Past.Year.12.17. The last three columns were renamed to”Rates_12_17”, “Rates_18_25”, and “Mari_12_17” respectively.
“State” is character data that includes all 50 states plus the District of Columbia.
“Year” is integer data that spanned from 2002 to 2018, though we filtered down to only leave 2018, 2013, and 2008.
“Rates_12_17”, “Rates_18_25”, and “Mari_12_17” are all integer data that was calculated in the original dataset from a Totals column and a Population column. We decided to remove both of these columns. The 12_17 and 18_25 indicate age range.


Show code
# Create vectors by isolating data that fits our criteria for Population A (12-17)
PopA <- d |> 
  filter(Year == "2018" | Year == "2013" | Year == "2008") |>
  select(c(
    State, 
    Year, 
    Rates.Alcohol.Use.Disorder.Past.Year.12.17, 
    Rates.Alcohol.Use.Disorder.Past.Year.18.25, 
    Rates.Marijuana.Used.Past.Year.12.17
    )
  ) # Select the columns of interest

# Felix 3-5-25

# AUD (Alcohol Use Disorder) [for ages 12-17]
# Added Mari_12_17 - rates of marijuana use in past year for same age group
AUD <- tibble( # Create tibble AUD, set variables
  State = PopA$State,
  Year = PopA$Year,
  Rates_12_17 = PopA$Rates.Alcohol.Use.Disorder.Past.Year.12.17,
  Rates_18_25 = PopA$Rates.Alcohol.Use.Disorder.Past.Year.18.25,
  Mari_12_17 = PopA$Rates.Marijuana.Used.Past.Year.12.17
)

AUD

title: Unit 1 Project - Alcohol Use Disorder in Adolescents
text:
Visualization

Fig. A) Comparing alcohol use between all 50 states
Alayna - My part in the Unit 1 Group Project’s visualizations was to create a histogram. The coding I used was imported using the read.csv () command and was made to include the years 2008, 2013, and 2018. The column titled “Rates.Alcohol.Use.Disorder.Past.Year.18.25” was changed to “Rates” for clarity. The x-axis symbolized the rates variable that acted for the percentage of 18-25 years olds with alcohol use disorder, while the y-axis represented the state variable that was reorganized based on the Rates. The fill aesthetic was based on the year variable, making the different years different shades of green (pale green, lime green, and forest green). The geom_histogram(stat = “identity”) command displayed the alcohol use disorder rates as bars. Position = “dodge” ensured that the bars for the years (2008, 2013, 2018) were placed next to each other for comparison. The width = 0.7 command was used to help with the bars’ visibility.


Show code
df <- read.csv("data/drugs.csv")

df_filtered <- df %>%
  filter (Year %in% c(2008, 2013, 2018)) %>%
  select(State, Year, Rates = 'Rates.Alcohol.Use.Disorder.Past.Year.18.25')

ggplot(df_filtered, aes(
  x = Rates, 
  y = reorder(State, Rates), 
  fill = factor (Year))) +
  geom_histogram(stat = "identity", position = "dodge", width = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "Alcohol Use Disorder Rates (Ages 18-25) by State",
       y = "States",
       x = "Rates (%)",
       fill = "Year",
       caption = "Data from corgis.edu") +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "2008" = "palegreen", 
      "2013" = "limegreen", 
      "2018" = "forestgreen")
    ) +
  theme_minimal (base_size = 8) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(
    angle = 360, 
    hjust = 1, 
    vjust = 0.5, 
    size = 4.7)
  )











Fig. B) Comparing national alcohol use rates over time
Emry - I was tasked with creating a temporal visualization showcasing the three years we chose. To do this, I mapped Year to the x-axis and rates to the y-axis. I knew that I wanted to use a jitter plot since there were only three values along the x axis, though I did not know how to represent both age groups. I spoke with Dr. Girard and he introduced the pivot_longer command. I also added a geom_smooth line to show the trend of the datapoints for each age group. Beyond this, the rest of the code was added for aesthetic purposes, such as sizing and cleaning up the scale.


Show code
#Create tibble, join rates columns into column Age_Group with new column rates
AUD2 <-
  AUD |> 
  pivot_longer(
    Rates_12_17:Rates_18_25,
    names_to = "Age_Group",
    values_to = "Rate"
  )

#Create+format figure showing rate by year with both age groups colored coded

ggplot(AUD2, aes(x = Year, y = Rate, color = Age_Group)) + geom_jitter() + 
  scale_color_discrete(
    labels = c("Rates_12_17" = "12-17", "Rates_18_25" = "18-25")) + 
  labs(title = "AUD rates in USA, District of Columbia by age over time") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(2008, 2013, 2018)) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 10) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  labs(color = "Age Group") +
  labs(caption = "Data from corgis.edu")









Show code
# Scale shows three years we are focusing on
# Sizing changed so title is not cut off
# Linear regression added to demonstrate trend
# Emry 3.11.25




Fig. C) Comparing alcohol use between age groups and states: KS and MO
Sev - The process of deciding on my figure was a trial and error one. I first played around with the box plot but decided that it was not as clear. Then I transitioned into a violin plot, but it was still confusing to look at visually. Then I came to the final conclusion of using the density plot because it was really easy to see visually. One of the things that I struggled with was putting all of the data onto the density plot. The way I combatted this was making one of the data points “Ages 18-25” a dashed line so it was easier to distinguish between them. Asked Dr. Girard on how to make it look visually better and he used pivot_longer and helped us with the code.


Show code
## Filters the dataset for Kansas and Missouri
PopB <- d |> 
  filter(State == "Kansas" | State == "Missouri") |> 
  select(
    State, 
    Year, 
    Rates.Alcohol.Use.Disorder.Past.Year.12.17, 
    Rates.Alcohol.Use.Disorder.Past.Year.18.25) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(
      Rates.Alcohol.Use.Disorder.Past.Year.18.25, 
      Rates.Alcohol.Use.Disorder.Past.Year.12.17), 
    names_to = "Age", 
    values_to = "Rate",
    names_prefix = "Rates.Alcohol.Use.Disorder.Past.Year."
  )

ggplot(PopB) +
  # Plots ages 12-17
  geom_density(aes(x = Rate, color = State, linetype = Age), linewidth = 2) +
  labs(
    x = "Rates of Alcohol Use Ages 12-17 & Dashed Line = 18-25.",
    y = "Density (Different rates of alcohol use disorder KS & MO)",
    fill = "State",
    color = "State"
  )









Show code
# Sev 3.12.25




Fig D) Comparing Alcohol Use and Marijuana Use
Felix - For this graph I wanted to see if there was a correlation between alcohol use and marijuana use in the past year for the 12-17 age group. I tried a couple of different plots, but a simple scatterplot was the best approach to look at two continuous variables. I added a smooth so that it would show the mean trend line. To differentiate the years that we isolated (2008, 2013, 2018), I set both color and shape to be mapped to the year.


Show code
# Creates a scatterplot comparing rates of alcohol use and marijuana use in the past year for ages 12-17

ggplot(AUD, 
  aes(
    y = Rates_12_17, 
    x = Mari_12_17, 
    color = factor(Year), 
    shape = factor(Year)
    )
  ) + 
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth() +
  labs(
    title = "Substance use in ages 12-17 in the USA",
    subtitle = "2008 shows some correlation between alcohol use and marijuana use, others show little effect",
    caption = "Data from CORGIS Dataset Project",
    
    x = "Rates of Marijuana Use in Past Year",
    y = "Rates of Alcohol Use in Past Year",
    
    color = "Year",
    shape = "Year"
  )









Show code
# Felix 3-12-25

title: Unit 1 Project - Alcohol Use Disorder in Adolescents
text:
Conclusions
Based on our graphs, we can see that alcohol use in the 12-17 age category across the country was on the decline during the 10-year period that we observed. We can also see that both Kansas and Missouri show similar trends in alcohol use between the 12-17 and 18-25 age groups. This trend is supported on the national level, where we see a higher density of high-rate alcohol use in the 18-25 age category than the 12-17, which makes sense due to national liquor laws. As for correlating alcohol use with another drug, like marijuana, we did not see a significant connection between the two.
For conclusions based on individual figures, see the following:

Fig. A - Alayna
The histogram shows that Montana had the largest alcohol use disorder rate in 2008 (245.91%), while Utah had the smallest alcohol use disorder rate in 2008 (124.2%). New Hampshire had the largest alcohol use disorder rate in 2013 (171.7%), while Louisiana had the smallest alcohol use disorder rate in 2013 (101.306%). New Hampshire had the highest alcohol use disorder rate in 2018 (143.95%), while Florida had the smallest alcohol use disorder rate in 2018 (71.22%).


Fig. B - Emry
The age range of 18-25 has higher overall rates of Alcohol Use Disorder rates. However, though both age groups see a downturn in rates across time, 18-25 sees a steeper negative regression slope.


Fig. C - Sev
This graph shows that for people ages 12-17, the consumption of alcohol was greater in Kansas for the majority of the time. For people ages 18-25, the consumption of alcohol was greater in Kansas as well with a big change at around 180 rates.


Fig. D - Felix
This graph shows that there are no major correlations between alcohol use and marijuana use for the 12-17 age group for the years that we selected. 2008 shows a greater slope, suggesting a correlation, but the points vary significantly, so this could be due to outlier influence. Overall, we can see that rates of alcohol use throughout the US decreased over 10 years, with marijuana use remaining relatively steady, with some states reporting greater use, and some with less.

title: Unit 2 Group Project
text: For our project, we looked at IMDB’s top 1000 movies (Chong) and each chose a different relationship to analyse to delve deeper.

title: Unit 2 Group Project
text: For our project, we looked at IMDB’s top 1000 movies (Chong) and each chose a different relationship to analyse to delve deeper.

title: Unit 2 Group Project
text:
Data Wrangling


Loading libraries (tidyverse, dplyr, patchwork)
# Load libraries
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)
library(patchwork)




Loading data files
## Load files
imdbClean <- read_csv("Data/imdb_clean.csv") |> 
  rename(gross_m = 'gross(M)')
imdbRaw <- read_csv("Data/imdb_raw.csv")




Summarize data
## Summarize by Genres
imdbGenres <- imdbClean |>
  dplyr::summarize(
    genreTotal = sum(gross_m, na.rm = TRUE),
    gross = (gross_m),
    films = n_distinct(title, na.rm = TRUE),
    .by = genre
  )

## Summarize by Metascores
imdb_meta_na <- imdbClean|>
  mutate(metascore = na_if(metascore, 0))

genre_metascore <- dplyr::summarize(
  imdbClean,
  .by = genre,
  mmetascore = mean(metascore)) |>
  arrange(desc(mmetascore))

## Sumamrize by Gross Revenue
imdb_gross_na <- imdbClean |> 
  mutate(
    gross = if_else(`gross_m` == 0, NA, `gross_m`))

director_summary <- imdb_gross_na |> 
  group_by(director) |> 
  dplyr::summarize(
    avg_rating = mean(rating, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_gross = mean(gross, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_movies = n())


To find our dataset, our group went through Kaggle to find something that caught our eye. We decided we wanted to look at movies, so after debating several datasets, we settled on “IMDB Top 1000 Movies”. The dataset was already cleaned, so we did not have to do much as far as wrangling, but we did change the name of “gross(M)” to avoid complications with parentheses and changed metascore values of zero because these films did not have pages on metacritic, where this score comes from. From there, we used summarize to calculate the total gross revenue, the number of films per genre, and mean metascores by genre.
Each group member chose a relationship they wanted to consider from there.

title: Unit 2 Group Project
text:
Visualizations

Examining the Top 10 Movies by Gross Revenue by Alayna C.


Alayna’s code
df_unique <- imdbClean %>%
  distinct(title, gross_m, .keep_all = TRUE)
top10_grossing <- df_unique %>%
  arrange(desc(gross_m)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 10)
ggplot(top10_grossing, aes(x = reorder(title, gross_m), y = gross_m)) +
  geom_point(size = 1.5) +
  labs(title = "Top 10 Grossing Movies by Box Office Income",
       x = "Movie Title",
       y = "Gross Income (in Millions)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))









My part in the Unit 2 Project was to create a ggplot based on the top 10 grossing movies in terms of box office income (movies ~ gross_m). I tidied my dataset using the distinct() code to ensure the movie titles aligned with their gross income. Next, I used arrange() and slice_head() to separate the top 10 highest-grossing movies from the rest of the dataset. For the visual aid, I made a scatterplot using ggplot2. I mapped the movie titles to the x-axis and the gross income to the y-axis. The results show which movies had the highest box office income. I also used minimal theming and angled my x-axis movie titles


Examining Metascore Ratings by Genre by Emry L.


Emry’s code
top_5_metascore <- 
  imdb_meta_na |>
  filter(genre %in% c("Western", "Animation", "Film-Noir", "War", "Sci-Fi", "Thriller", "Comedy", "Crime", "Action", "Musical")) |> 
  mutate(
    genre = factor(genre, c("Western", "Animation", "Film-Noir", "War", "Sci-Fi", "Thriller", "Comedy", "Crime", "Action", "Musical"))
  )

ggplot(top_5_metascore, aes(x = metascore, y = rating, color = genre)) + 
  geom_point(na.rm = TRUE) + geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1/10) +
  facet_wrap(~genre) +
  labs(
    title = "Metascore by User Rating Across Genres",
    x = "User Rating", 
    y = "Metascore (Critic Rating)"
  )









For my code, I looked at the metascore (a number created by a site called metacritic that looks at critic reviews, taking into consideration said critic’s reputation) against IMDB user ratings by genre. I used the summary of mean metascore by genre to find the average highest five and lowest five ranked genres, and focused on these 10 genres for my visualization so as to not overwhelm the viewer.
I used factoring to reorder the data so it would come across in order when I faceted the data. I also decided to use geom_abline to add a line through the visualizations with a slope of 1/10 to make it easier to compare the scores since metascore is out of 100 and rating is out of 10 and uses decimals.
From this visualization, I found that critics and the common viewer overall feel relatively similarly about film genres. However, IMDB users ranked Film-Noir, War, and Animation higher than critics. Critics rated Action, Sci-Fi, and Crime higher than users.


Examining Top Directors by Gross Revenue by Sev T.


Sev’s code
top_directors <- director_summary |> 
  arrange(desc(avg_rating)) |> 
  filter(row_number() <= 15)
top_directors |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = avg_rating, y = avg_gross)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_text(aes(label = director), vjust = -0.5, size = 2.8) + 
  labs(
    title = "Top 15 Directors by Average Rating",
    x = "Average IMDb Rating",
    y = "Average Gross (in millions)"
  )









The purpose of my code was to look at each of the top 15 directors that made the most gross money. Some of the data had missing data so I replaced the 0 with NA so it would not be included in the calculations. Then I grouped the data to director to filter it. Then, I found each director’s average imdb rating because I wanted to get the top 15 directors by rating. I also averaged their gross earnings & movies because some of the directors made more than one movie and this would be a fair way to see how much money they make on average. After I did that, I made it select the top 15 directors with the highest imdb rating using the filter command. Then I plotted their average using ggplot geompoint. The x axis was the average imdb rating of the director and the y axis was the average gross these actors made which was in millions.


Examining Gross Revenue by Genre by Felix G.


Felix’s code
imdbGenres1 <- imdbGenres |>
  mutate(genre = fct_reorder(genre, films, .desc = TRUE)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = genre, y = gross)) + geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    title = "IMDB's top 1000 movies: Gross revenue per genre",
    x = "Film genre (in order of total films)",
    y = "Gross revenue in millions ($)"
  ) +
  stat_summary(
    fun = "mean",
    geom = "point",
    color = "blue"
  ) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )

imdbGenres2 <- imdbGenres |>
  mutate(genre = fct_reorder(genre, genreTotal, .desc = TRUE)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = genre, y = gross)) + geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    x = "Film genre (in order of total gross revenue)",
    y = "Gross revenue in millions ($)"
  ) +
  stat_summary(
    fun = "mean",
    geom = "point",
    color = "blue"
  ) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )

imdbGenres1 / imdbGenres2









I wanted to focus on gross revenue for the genres to see whether genre is a good predictor of gross revenue. I chose a boxplot for this, as it shows the interquartile range and clearly shows outliers.
I then added a stat_summary to show the mean. As we can see, the mean is higher the the median (50th percentile) in almost every genre, which suggests that the distribution is skewed towards higher revenue. On the surface we can see that the genres Adventure, Action, Sci-Fi, Animation, and Fantasy show significantly higher revenue than other genres.
Finally, I used Patchwork to display both versions of the graph: ordered by total number of films, and ordered by total gross revenue.

title: Unit 2 Group Project
text:
Future Directions
We discovered multiple shortcomings with this dataset.
First, there were many films with a metacritic score of 0, which we interpreted as missing data. To fix this, we converted those entries to NA to remove them for the metascore analysis.
Second, the top 15 directors showed a significant overlapping grouping around the origin point of the graph, suggesting less variance between directors with lower scores. To alleviate this, we decided to remove those data point from the graph to highlight the top 7 directors with greater variance between scores.
Finally, many of the films in this list have multiple genres. Therefore, the gross revenue of a single film could be added to multiple genres. For example, Star Wars: The Force Awakens is Action, Adventure, and Sci-FI, meaning that its $936.66 million is added to all three genres total gross figure. All of the top 10 movies by gross revenue have 2 or more genres. The resulting graph suggests that Adventure, Action, Sci-Fi, Animation, and Fantasy produce higher gross revenue, but it is likely that many blockbuster films overlap in these common genres.
To alleviate this, we recommend that future analysis should include Principal Component Analysis to discover factor loadings associated with genre.

title: Machine and Statistical Learning final project
text: About one out of every three adults in the United States have reported not seeking health care because of the cost (Sparks et. al. 2026). I explored whether we could predict whether individuals would avoid seeking medical care based on health status, socioeconomic, and demographic indicators. I utilized publicly available data to train machine learning models in a classification framework. This research hopes to test the validity of predictive modeling on this topic as well as gain additional insights into this phenomenon.

title: Machine and Statistical Learning final project
text: About one out of every three adults in the United States have reported not seeking health care because of the cost (Sparks et. al. 2026). I explored whether we could predict whether individuals would avoid seeking medical care based on health status, socioeconomic, and demographic indicators. This research hopes to test the validity of predictive modeling on this topic as well as gain additional insights into this phenomenon.

title: Machine and Statistical Learning final project
text:
Data Description and Preprocessing
Data was collected from the Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System database maintained by the CDC. This is an annual telephone survey that focuses on health status and healthcare utilization metrics across the United States. Data used in this project was harmonized from the 2017-2024 survey years, resulting in about 3.5 million observations before preprocessing. Table 1 shows main variable groups for prediction (for full data dictionary, see appendix - table a1)

Table A. Main variable grouping







Group
Type(s)
Variables




Outcome

MEDCOST


Demographic indicators
Categorical binary and factor
X_SEX; X_AGE_G; X_IMPRACE;


Socioeconomic indicators
Categorical binary and factor
X_EDUCAG; X_INCOMG; PERSDOC


Disability indicators
Binary - True/False
DEAF; BLIND; DECIDE; DIFFWALK; DIFFDRES; DIFFALON


Chronic Health Condition (CHC) indicators
Binary - True/False
CVDINFR; CVDCRHD; CVDSTRK; ASTHNOW; CHCCOPD; HAVART; ADDEPEV; DIABETE



Due to high frequency of missing data in predictors, I implemented a one-hot coding method to impute and track observations with missing values in binary predictors. Then, dropping missing rows removed 500,401 rows, resulting in 2,966,543 rows in the final dataset. All variables were categorical and were therefore factored and labeled accordingly. Exploratory data analysis shows the severe class imbalance on the outcome variable with only 8% of observations being true positive cases (Figure 1).

title: Machine and Statistical Learning final project
text:
Methods
I tested four models using a caret workflow: a ridge and lasso penalized regression for feature selection and shrinkage, a decision tree as an interpretive baseline, and random forest to reduce overfitting and improve generalizability over the decision tree.
3-fold cross validation with a train/test split was conducted on all models except for the random forest, due to computational restrictions. As a result of this, the random forest model was evaluated using out-of-bag predictions. Hyperparameter tuning was conducted on all models to identify the best parameters based on ROC AUC performance.

title: Machine and Statistical Learning final project
text:
Results
All models showed moderate ranking ability between both cross-validated and hold-out data (ROC AUC = 0.773-0.817). Balanced accuracy was selected as the primary evaluation metric to account for the severe class imbalance in the outcome variable. Sensitivity and specificity were included to evaluate discrimination between cases. (Table 1)

names(rf_comparison) <- gsub("OOB_", "cv_", names(rf_comparison))

Figure 2: ML model performance across key metrics













Figure 3: Discrepancy between models on train-test balanced accuracy stability





The Random Forest model is the best performing model based on balanced accuracy at 0.741. However, the Ridge and Lasso models both performed within 0.001 and 0.002 points respectively, so the improvement that the RF model introduces is marginal (Table 1). Additionally, the RF model was not subjected to 3-fold cross validation like the other models so performance was based on out-of-bag predictions, which leads to more optimistic results. Because the Random Forest model did not significantly improve performance over Ridge or Lasso models enough to justify the extra model complexity, Ridge is the best model.


Show code
cm_df <- read_csv("ridge_cm_df.csv")

ggplot(cm_df, aes(Reference, Prediction, fill = value)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = value), color = "white") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "grey60", high = "firebrick") +
  labs(title = "Ridge GLM confusion matrix at threshold = 0.08")








Figure 4: Heatmap of the ridge confusion matrix shows predicted outcomes





The model displays moderate recall ability (Sens. = 0.72) but poor precision (0.21) due to the low threshold (0.08) classifying far more cases as likely to avoid care than the default baseline threshold of 0.5. This results in moderately few false negative cases but a high proportion of false positives. A specificity of 0.757 shows moderate performance on predictive negative cases correctly. Due in part to the severe class imbalance and large prevalence of negative cases, the negative predictive value (NPV) is 0.968. This shows that the model is more likely to predict false positives than false negatives. Reducing the probability of false negatives aligns with the goal of not missing vulnerable individuals in predictions, which in turn allows for improved screening and timely interventions. (Figure 4)

Figure 5: Variable importance plot shows strongest predictors





Variable importance shows a number of plausible predictors. The presence of a health plan is the most influential variable for the ridge model. Other socioeconomic and demographic variables, such as age over 65 years and annual income over $200,000 also extend above 50% relative importance, signifying the presence of a signal along those variables. The next bracket, between 25% and 50% relative importance, includes whether one has a personal doctor or primary care physician (PERSDOC_imp) and the prior income bracket (X_INCOMG$100k-200k). It also introduces our first health status indicators for poor majority mental and physical health days (X_MENT14D14+ days; X_PHYS13D14+ days), as well as majority good mental and physical health days (X_MENT14D1-13 days; X_PHYS14D1-13 days). The next age group we see (X_AGE_G25-34) is also in the 15% to 50% bracket. While we cannot infer causality nor magnitude of relationship from the variable importance graph, these findings are consistent with common perceptions about how different age and socioeconomic groups engage with healthcare. (Figure 5)

title: Machine and Statistical Learning final project
text:
Ethics & Fairness Considerations
This research exists within the public health and policy domains, but intended use of this research remains strictly educational. Results herein do not imply causality between the phenomena examined. Future works should address the methodological and ethical concerns addressed here.
The data sourced for this research is a publicly-available and anonymized dataset hosted by the CDC (see references for attribution). This data utilizes iterative proportional fitting, also known as raking, to weight survey data representatively to existing proportions of demographic identity within a population. Weighted variables were not used in the development of predictive models and may not be sufficiently representative of individual populations. This is a remaining limitation of this research, and it introduces a bias and fairness risk. This was mitigated by research design avoiding drawing inferences about populations at scale. While the data is depersonalized, there exists a marginal reidentification risk due to the data containing information about demographics, identity, and socioeconomic and health status. To reduce this risk, only summary and aggregate data (confusion matrices, statistical results) were reported.
Microsoft Copilot was used for methodological research, code scaffolding and debugging. Lack of methodological experience introduced a risk of over-reliance on LLM output. This was mitigated by direct code validation and testing by the user as well as cross-reference output with R documentation, course materials, and other sources. No copyrighted material nor raw data was input into the AI assistant, and only summary, aggregate, or results data and original code was used in interpretive or debugging tasks.

title: Machine and Statistical Learning final project
text:
Discussion
This research shows that it is possible to predict who is vulnerable to avoid healthcare due to cost from publicly available data. The results indicate that there is a moderate signal that was identified, but further precision could not be attained through model tuning alone. Although the random forest model is far more complex, it did not meaningfully outperform the ridge model. Noise in the signal could stem from unobserved covariates or an inherently weak signal between the predictors and outcome variable.
Another limitation of this project was the scale of the data used in model training. The use of a large dataset introduced a number of computational limitations, and as such, this project lacked a baseline GLM to compare machine learning models against, as well as 3-fold cross-validation on the random forest model. This negatively impacts the validity of this research by weakening the methodological rigor.
Future work should address these limitation by reducing the scale of the data, potentially with a more narrow geographic or temporal scope, to enable more rigorous and thorough cross validation techniques. Additionally, future research should narrow the scope of predictive modeling to variables that display a stronger connection to the phenomenon, such as socioeconomic predictors.

title: Machine and Statistical Learning final project
text:
Conclusion
This research shows that comparably simple models like penalized regression can compete with more complex models like random forests on predictive performance. The random forest model showed marginal improvement over the ridge model, the computational and interpretive complexity of the model is not justified by such minor gains.
Through this project, I learned about how to use a caret workflow to cross validate, tune, and train models, as well as how to interpret model performance on new data. I also learned a lot of hard lessons about research design - particularly how I sourced data, developed a research framework, and how the size of data can significantly impact the scope of the project.

title: Can we predict rising unmet health need?
text:
Sponsored by Pradeep Kumar of the Industry Advisory Board, this project asks, “can we predict the rise of unmet health needs in adults with disabilities?” Adults with disabilities struggle with access to healthcare services more than the general populace, including geographic isolation, socioeconomic challenges, and systemic inequities. In order to reduce the risk of widening disparities of unmet health need, poor health outcomes, and vulnerability to chronic conditions, we utilized machine learning to predict which states across the country were expected to have an increase in unmet health needs across the disabled adult population using publicly available health survey data from the Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) dataset.
I was inundated with unknowns as I faced this question. This was my first time designing a project around machine learning and I had a period of adjustment as I navigated what that would require. I started by building my dataset, a process that expanded as I iterated through harmonization methods. The Long Term Ecological Research Project (LTER) has a package that provides a useful framework for data harmonization, which I used extensively in the early stages of this project as I iterated on the workflow. I chose to combine data from 2017 to 2024. After initial harmonization, I continued to iterate on the overall research design. With much gratitude to guidance from course mentors, I settled on a feature set could codify observations based on disability status, poor health status, and access to healthcare. These were in turn used to create our final proxy after aggregation to a state-year panel that tracked the percentage of the disable adult population in each state that had unmet health needs. I obtained a dataset from the University of Kentucky Center for Poverty Research that contained state-level socioeconomic demographics and policy data. These would become my predictors in the upcoming classification task.
The final analysis took the form of a model evaluation comparison between five machine learning algorithms: a baseline pooled OLS regression with state-clustered SEs, a ridge and a lasso penalized logistic regression, a decision tree, and a random forest model. I intended to use this framework to ensure that I would capture a signal and could gain key insights like which states had the highest proportion of vulnerable adults, and which states were expected to increase. I also wanted to determine which predictors were the most plausible predictors that could inform future research through variable importance.

title: Can we predict rising unmet health need?
text:
Sponsored by Pradeep Kumar of the Industry Advisory Board, this project asks, “can we predict the rise of unmet health needs in adults with disabilities?” Adults with disabilities struggle with access to healthcare services more than the general populace, including geographic isolation, socioeconomic challenges, and systemic inequities. In order to reduce the risk of widening disparities of unmet health need, poor health outcomes, and vulnerability to chronic conditions, we utilized machine learning to predict which states across the country were expected to have an increase in unmet health needs across the disabled adult population using publicly available health survey data from the Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) dataset.
I was inundated with unknowns as I faced this question. This was my first time designing a project around machine learning and I had a period of adjustment as I navigated what that would require. I started by building my dataset, a process that expanded as I iterated through harmonization methods. The Long Term Ecological Research Project (LTER) has a package that provides a useful framework for data harmonization, which I used extensively in the early stages of this project as I iterated on the workflow. I chose to combine data from 2017 to 2024. After initial harmonization, I continued to iterate on the overall research design. With much gratitude to guidance from course mentors, I settled on a feature set could codify observations based on disability status, poor health status, and access to healthcare. These were in turn used to create our final proxy after aggregation to a state-year panel that tracked the percentage of the disable adult population in each state that had unmet health needs. I obtained a dataset from the University of Kentucky Center for Poverty Research that contained state-level socioeconomic demographics and policy data. These would become my predictors in the upcoming classification task.
The final analysis took the form of a model evaluation comparison between five machine learning algorithms: a baseline pooled OLS regression with state-clustered SEs, a ridge and a lasso penalized logistic regression, a decision tree, and a random forest model. I intended to use this framework to ensure that I would capture a signal and could gain key insights like which states had the highest proportion of vulnerable adults, and which states were expected to increase. I also wanted to determine which predictors were the most plausible predictors that could inform future research through variable importance.

title: Can we predict rising unmet health need?
text:
Data
Data collection occurred in two stages. The first centered on developing our predictor variable from data from The Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS), the second concerned obtaining our state-level predictors from the University of Kentucky Center for Poverty Research (UKCPR).
The BRFSS is an annual phone survey conducted by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and covers demographic and socioeconomic information, health status, and health behaviors. As previously mentioned, I first harmonized data from 2017-2024. As the online database contains data in XPT format for SAS, I first wrote a script to batch convert the data files from XPT to CSV for analysis in R. I selected data back to 2017 for convenience and in anticipation of computational limitations. The current iteration of BRFSS methodology is compatible as far back as the 2011 survey year, and the harmonization methods I utilized could extend that far as well. The BRFSS dataset is quite extensive, including raw survey responses and calculated and imputed values. When possible, I deferred to calculated variables to match existing methodological standards. Feature selection was an iterative process as I became familiar with and accustomed to the BRFSS documentation. In the end, I included survey weighting and design variables, demographic information, health status indicators, and health behavior indicators. After finalizing harmonization and generating my final dataset, I had 50 variables and nearly 3.5 million observations. This would reduce as I narrowed the scope and refined my research design. For a full list of the included variables, see supplement Final_panel_dict.csv. *Supplement 2.6 - BRFSS Data Wrangling Automation R file availble on request.**
Before analysis, the dataset was extensively cleaned and features were converted to factors. BRFSS data has multiple forms of missing data indicators, with different indicators for unsure responses, refusal to respond, and simply missing or unrecorded responses. The calculated variables from the BRFSS dataset largely did not have this issue, with such responses already collapsed to a single missing code. However, key engineered features would rely on majority raw data, so missing codes were manually converted for all features that needed it. As all non-design variables were categorical or binary, they were also factored and labeled accordingly at this stage.
After factoring all categorical variables, I designed key indicators that I would use to construct the outcome variable. I aggregated six disability indicators into DIS_ANY (presence of any disability) and eight chronic health condition indicators into CHC_ANY (presence of any chronic health conditions). As these were binary variables, I took the opportunity to use a one-hot coding scheme to impute missing values with missingness indicators. This allowed me to maintain statistical power by losing far fewer observations from dropping rows with missing values. I calculated HLTH_NEED, which is a binary indicator of whether an individual had some form of health need, such as chronic health conditions or reported poor general health. LACK_ACS is another binary feature that denotes a lack of health insurance, no personal doctor, or delayed care due to cost. HLTH_NEED and LACK_ACS then created UNMET_NEED (the presence of any health needs while lacking any form of access to healthcare). I then combined UNMET_NEED with DIS_ANY to show whether an observation was a part of the population that was the target of this research. This feature would allow us to calculate the proportion of the disabled population for each state-year that had unmet health needs relative to those that did not.
With feature engineering completed, I then summarized the data by state and year to get the summary statistics that show us the percentage of the disabled population per state-year that had unmet health needs. To maintain representativeness of the data, I used the BRFSS weighting variable _LLCPWT in the summary calculation. This is part of the raking method that is used in the BRFSS study, which is calculated from existing state demographic data to ensure that studies like this produce results that are representative of the demographic makeup of a given state. Figure 1 demonstrates this by showing the percentage of disabled adults with unmet health needs per state in 2023.
The second stage of data collection involved the UKCPR dataset, which spans 1980 to 2024 and contains information about state socioeconomic status and state policy, such as minimum wage and Supplemental Security Income (SSI) participation (University of Kentucky, 2026). As both datasets were in a compatible state-year panel format, I selected the predictors of interest and joined the data. See Figure 6 for list of features.
Exploratory data analysis uncovered some insights. First, tracking the national average percentage of unmet health needs in the target population shows a significant decrease from 2019 to 2021, from just under 35% of the population to just over 25% experiencing unmet health needs before increasing again through 2024 (Figure 2). This is likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, and could have resulted from a number of factors, such as shifting healthcare practices and community response, mortality rates among vulnerable populations, or socioeconomic assistance such as stimulus checks. This foreshadowed that national-level shocks would likely have a significant impact on our outcome variable as an unobserved covariate.
A scale and reliability check revealed a quirk of the data – the 2024 year of the BRFSS survey had continued into 2025. As a result, there were 15 states for 2025 that had less than 50 observations before aggregation (Figure 3), which inflated the outcome variable significantly (Figure 4). Additionally, the predictor set pulled from the UKCPR dataset only had records into 2024. 2025 was therefore dropped from the dataset due to being incomplete by design.
In order to facilitate prediction of change across years, I calculated whether each year showed an increase in the proportion of the disabled population with unmet health needs over the prior year as increased_dis_unmet_need. This set up the classification problem that would allow us to predict the rise of unmet health needs across the country.
Supplement 2.7 - BRFSS Feature Engineering and EDA QMD document is available upon request.

title: Can we predict rising unmet health need?
text:
Results
Before training models, I conducted a Hausman Test of Consistency to determine whether state fixed effects or random effects would be more appropriate. The Hausman Test failed to reject the null hypothesis of consistency of the random-effects estimator (chi^2(13) = 17.95, p = 0.16), indicating that random effects are consistent and more efficient. However, to maintain methodological consistency with machine learning workflows, state fixed effects will still be used for robustness.
A PLM with state fixed effects was conducted to check for direction, magnitude, and stability of effects within the predictor set. It revealed two primary effects. A state’s unemployment rate showed a minor negative effect with a stable signal (B = -0.09, |t| = 6.06, p < 0.001), indicating that as unemployment rate increases, the chance that a state will increase in unmet health needs decreases. Total supplemental breakfast program (SBP) participation showed a moderate positive effect on the outcome with a stable signal (B = 1.08e-6, |t| = 4.55, p < 0.001). While additional coefficients were not significant, this does not imply a lack of relationship and could be due to lower within-state change over year or between-state interactions like national shocks. Overall, the model shows strong relevance of predictors (F = 16.30, p < 0.001) and moderately meaningful explanatory power for within-state changes across years (r^2 = 0.23).
All models followed the same formula, predicting Increased_dis_unmet_need from 13 state policy and socioeconomic predictors as utilized in the Hausman Test and PLM prior. Before training machine learning models, a train-test split was created by holding out the final year of the data (2024). 5-fold state-grouped cross validation with upsampling and preprocessing (scale, center) was conducted on the training split to reduce the risk of intra-state leakage. Thresholds were tuned on CV probabilities and evaluated using ROC AUC. I used a caret workflow to train and test the models, ensuring consistent methodology between models. The pooled OLS model was conducted outside of the caret framework, and as such required manual preprocessing. While this model was cross validated by the same train/test split as the other models, it did not receive k-fold tuning.
The pooled OLS regression with state-clustered SEs acted as a baseline to compare predictive power against. This model had a sensitivity of 0.72 and a specificity of 0.07 on the hold-out test set. This trade off benefits the goal of predicting the increase of unmet health needs as we want the most opportunities to plan interventions. However, this model performed poorly on balanced accuracy. A balanced accuracy of 0.39 indicates that this model performed poorly. Balanced accuracy below 0.50 is considered to be less effective than random guessing for predicting cases. (Figure 5)
The cross validated ridge model showed an ROC AUC of 0.704 at a threshold of 0.51, which decreased to 0.574 on the held-out test set. Model sensitivity increased from 0.746 on CV to 0.889 on hold-out, suggesting that it selected the positive case more on the final test. Mode specificity decreased significantly from 0.628 to 0.067, suggesting that there were far more false positive flagged by this model in the test than the CV. This is further reflected by balanced accuracy, which dropped from 0.687 in the CV set to 0.478 in the held-out set. The lasso model showed similar trends to the ridge model. At a threshold of 0.49, ROC AUC dropped from 0.703 (CV) to 0.55 (hold-out). Sensitivity inflated more than on the ridge model, jumping from 0.754 on the CV set to 0.917 on the hold-out set. Specificity dropped from 0.581 (CV) to 0.067 (hold-out). Based on balanced accuracy, the lasso model performed worse on the hold-out set (0.492) than on the CV set (0.668). Both penalized regression models show poor predictive performance. Balanced accuracy below 0.50 demonstrates that these models are systematically misclassifying classes. (Figure 5)
At a threshold of 0.38, the ROC AUC of the decision tree model dropped from 0.638 (CV) to 0.496 (hold-out). Sensitivity and specificity showed a more moderate shift between cross validated and hold-out performance on these metrics than the penalized regression models (Sens: CV 0.672 -> Hold-out 0.722; Spec: CV 0.57 -> Hold-out 0.4). This model performed better than the ridge and lasso on balanced accuracy (CV = 0.621, Held-out = 0.561), showing modest improvement over random guessing. The random forest model showed an ROC AUC of 0.701 on the CV set and a 0.656 on the held-out set at a threshold of 0.36. Sensitivity was stable compared to other models, only shifting 0.04 points between 0.821 (CV) and 0.861 (Held-out). Specificity dropped significantly between the CV set (0.5) and held-out set (0.133). The random forest model performed just as poorly on classification as the penalized regression models (Balanced accuracy: CV = 0.66, Held-out = 0.497), showing consistent patterns of a low signal-to-noise ratio or limited explanatory power of the models. (Figure 5)
Supplement 3.2 – BRFSS prediction model comparison QMD file available on request.

title: Can we predict rising unmet health need?
text:
Conclusions
While the decision tree model performed the best, it is important to recognize that the benefit in predictive accuracy over random guessing was only marginal at a difference of only 0.061 points. Due to the small sample size of the train and test data splits, it is likely that this marginal improvement could be due to change.
Although the PLM model showed significant effects between limited predictors and the increase of unmet health needs across the country, the magnitude of those effects were marginal. This, combined with the performance of the machine learning models, suggests that the feature set are not plausible predictors on their own, and unobserved within-state and between-state effects are likely introducing noise and obscuring the signal.
I would recommend a few methodological changes to address these concerns. First is handling the computational limitations of aggregating BRFSS data. Instead of aggregating individual level data in bulk through data harmonization, I would recommend aggregating each survey year first, then aggregating it into a state-year panel. This would alleviate restraints introduced by large-scale data and ensure that the full catalogue of available data (2011-present) could be utilized.
Next, I would also recommend expanding selected predictors and performing feature selection through a lasso regression or PCA analysis identify stronger correlates to use as predictors. This alone would improve predictive performance significantly.
Finally, I would address two concerns on the approach of machine learning. First, I suggest a regression approach rather than a classification approach. While classifying year-over-year increase is a simple and interpretable approach, it would likely be more insightful to predict the degree to which unmet health needs are expected to change. Second, assuming additional survey years are added to future research, I would change the train-test split from an all-but-one approach (train on 2017-2023, test on 2024) to a proportional split. I believe a 70/30 or 80/20 split would be appropriate given the limited set of possible years.

text:
Ethics and Fairness Considerations
This research exists within the public health and policy domains, but intended use of this research remains strictly educational. Results herein do not imply causality between the phenomena examined. Future works should address the methodological and ethical concerns addressed here.
The data sourced for this research is a publicly-available and anonymized dataset hosted by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention and the University of Kentucky Center for Poverty Research (see references for attribution). Due to data aggregation, there exists no risk for reidentification of participants of neither the BRFSS survey nor data aggregated by the UKCPR.
Microsoft Copilot was used for methodological research, code scaffolding and debugging. Lack of methodological experience introduced a risk of over-reliance on LLM output. This was mitigated by direct code validation and testing by the user as well as cross-reference output with R documentation, course materials, and other sources. No copyrighted material nor identifiable data was input into the AI assistant, and only summary, aggregate, or results data and original code was used in interpretive or debugging tasks.
